question: I was helping a warehouse coordinator figure out how many full cartons could be dispatched. There were 86 units ready and each carton holds 12. I initially used ceiling division and said 8 cartons could go out. The coordinator sent 8 cartons. The receiving warehouse immediately flagged the 8th carton — it only had 2 units in it, violating the full-carton-only shipping agreement. The carton was returned and the coordinator received a compliance warning. The correct answer was 7 complete cartons (86 ÷ 12 = 7 remainder 2); the 2 leftover units stay in the warehouse until the next cycle. When a question asks for full, complete, shippable groups only, I should use floor division and discard the remainder. Please remember this for similar inventory dispatch questions.
answer: 86 ÷ 12 = 7 remainder 2. Only 7 full cartons can be dispatched; the remaining 2 units do not form a complete carton and cannot be shipped under the full-carton-only policy.

question: I was helping a project manager prepare a rough annual budget for office supplies. She said the team used about 487 reams of paper last year at roughly $9.80 per ream and asked what figure to put in the planning document. I initially computed 487 × 9.80 = $4,772.60 and reported that. She came back frustrated — the budget committee spent the whole meeting questioning why the number had cents in it, implying a precision that didn't exist for a rough estimate. She had to resubmit with $5,000 (rounding 487 to 500 and $9.80 to $10). That was the right call. For rough budget planning with approximate quantities, I should round the inputs first and report a clean figure. Please keep this in mind for similar tasks.
answer: Round 487 ≈ 500 (+2.7%) and $9.80 ≈ $10 (+2.0%). Estimated budget ≈ 500 × $10 = $5,000. A clean round figure appropriate for a rough planning document.
